justification: All 6 rules classified correctly (BR-1,4,6 as invariant:true; BR-2,3,5 as invariant:false). Each rule has a detailed written justification that clearly distinguishes state integrity constraints from configurable policy parameters. Rules 2, 3, and 5 explicitly use 'configurable'/'tunable operational parameter'/'policy knob' reasoning. Grace period ambiguity is surfaced in the escalations section, addressing whether the 3-strike rule applies within the 14-day window. The configurable-vs-hardcoded recommendation is implicit in the justifications (calling them 'tunable' and 'configurable') rather than an explicit 'store as configuration' recommendation, but the intent is clearly conveyed.

observations.text: Perfect 6/6 classification accuracy with no misclassifications
Justifications are thorough and consistently apply the state-integrity vs. configurable-policy framework
BR-4 justification is especially strong — explains why reactivation would corrupt billing history, audit trails, and revenue recognition
BR-6 justification correctly frames trial uniqueness as analogous to a database unique constraint
Grace period ambiguity surfaced as a formal escalation with two options and a recommendation
Additional escalation about 'failed payment method' definition adds value beyond the rubric requirements
Edge cases section is comprehensive, covering scenarios like email aliases, ambiguous payment processor status, and Premium 3-strike interaction
No explicit recommendation to 'store rules 2, 3, 5 as configuration rather than hard-coded constants' — the reasoning implies it but never states it as an actionable recommendation

trials.justification: All 6 rules classified correctly (BR-1,4,6 invariant:true; BR-2,3,5 invariant:false). Each rule has detailed written justification that clearly distinguishes state integrity constraints from configurable business policy. Rules 2, 3, and 5 explicitly use 'configurable'/'tunable parameter' reasoning. Grace period ambiguity is surfaced in multiple places: escalation about tier downgrade during grace period, edge case about premium grace period behavior, and AC-6 covering what happens after 14 days expire. The configurable-vs-invariant distinction is systematically applied throughout. Minor gap: no single explicit recommendation to 'store policies as configuration rather than hard-coded constants,' though the justifications for BR-2/3/5 repeatedly describe them as tunable/configurable parameters, strongly implying this. The additional escalations (retry counter reset, trial lifetime vs concurrency) and edge cases go well beyond the minimum rubric requirements.

observations.text: All 6 invariant classifications are correct with no errors
Justifications are exceptionally detailed — each explains WHY it is or isn't an invariant using concrete reasoning (e.g., BR-1 compared to 'shipping to an invalid address')
Configurable vs state-integrity distinction is systematically applied across all 6 rules, not just mentioned once
Grace period ambiguity surfaced in escalation #3 (tier downgrade during grace period) and edge case (premium subscriber payment on day 13)
Went beyond rubric requirements — surfaced additional ambiguities (retry counter reset, trial 'at any time' interpretation) that are genuinely valuable
Each escalation includes structured options with a recommendation, showing domain reasoning depth
No explicit recommendation to store BR-2/3/5 values as configuration rather than hard-coded constants — the reasoning implies it but doesn't state it as an architectural recommendation

trials.justification: All 6 rules classified correctly: BR-1 (invariant: true), BR-2 (invariant: false), BR-3 (invariant: false), BR-4 (invariant: true), BR-5 (invariant: false), BR-6 (invariant: true). Each rule has detailed written justification distinguishing state integrity constraints from configurable policy parameters. Rules 2, 3, 5 explicitly use 'configurable' reasoning (tunable operational parameter, configurable policy threshold, tier-specific policy parameter). Grace period ambiguity surfaced in multiple places: edge cases discuss the 14-day boundary condition, escalations address whether premium tier has unlimited retries or a hybrid model, and confidence section flags the interaction between 3-strike and grace period. Configuration recommendation is implicit in the invariant: false justifications stating these values can be changed by ops/management.

observations.text: Perfect 6/6 classification accuracy with no misclassifications
Exceptionally detailed justifications that clearly articulate the state integrity vs. configurable policy distinction for every rule
Grace period ambiguity surfaced from multiple angles: attempt limits during grace period, day-14 boundary semantics, and interaction with 3-strike rule
Testable assertions provided for each rule, going beyond the rubric requirements
Edge cases are well-considered, including the premium tier 3rd-attempt scenario and trial email change abuse vector
Business impact analysis adds stakeholder context that strengthens the justifications

Could have been more explicit about recommending that rules 2, 3, 5 be stored as configuration rather than hard-coded constants — the reasoning supports it but the recommendation is not stated as a direct engineering action item
